title: Two-qubit charger-battery system subject to weak, continuous measurements with quantum point contacts
abstract: Quantum batteries modeled as two-qubit systems coupled to Markovian thermal reservoirs have been shown to benefit from measurement-assisted charging, where projective measurements enhance the charging rate at an infinite thermodynamic resource cost. In this work, we consider weak, continuous measurements implemented via quantum point contact detectors (QPC), which enhance the charging rate at a definite and quantifiable resource cost. We analyze three measurement configurations namely, a single QPC, two independent QPCs, and a series-coupled (coherent) two-QPC scheme, and study their effect on the steady-state charging rate, defined as the rate of energy flow from the charger qubit to the battery qubit, relative to the unmeasured baseline. We find that the charging rate enhancement is non-monotonic as a function of the temperature gradient and potential gradient required to drive the QPCs, exhibiting a plateau of near-optimal enhancement. Comparing the three configurations, the plateau of optimal enhancement contracts toward lower temperature and chemical potential for both the cases with two QPCs compared to the single QPC case. The coherent measurement further shows a lowering of the resource requirement relative to the two independent QPC case for achieving the same enhancement. The hierarchy is coherent greater than two independent QPCs which is greater than single QPC with respect to both the magnitude of the charging rate enhancement and the minimization of measurement resources.
summary: This theoretical study analyzes how weak, continuous measurements using Quantum Point Contacts (QPCs) enhance the energy charging rate in a two-qubit quantum battery. The authors compare single, independent, and coherently coupled measurement schemes. They find that implementing a coherent measurement across both qubits yields the optimal enhancement with the lowest associated resource cost.
main_problem: The paper investigates how weak, continuous quantum measurements enhance the charging rate of a quantum battery system while quantifying the associated thermodynamic resource cost.
main_result: A coherent measurement scheme coupling both the charger and battery qubits provides the highest charging rate enhancement (13.64%) while requiring the lowest resource expenditure compared to single or independent measurements.
method: The analysis uses the Born and Markov approximations to derive a Lindblad master equation incorporating measurement back-action from Quantum Point Contacts (QPCs).
model_or_system: The system is a two-qubit charger-battery model coupled to thermal reservoirs and subjected to weak, continuous measurements via QPCs. The dynamics are governed by a master equation incorporating measurement-induced dissipators and energy shifts.
key_observables: Charging rate enhancement ($\eta$), energy flow rate from charger to battery, and the resource cost (temperature/potential gradients) required for measurement.
important_parameters: Temperature gradient ($T_{	ext{meas}}/T_c$) and chemical potential gradient ($\mu_{	ext{meas}}/\Omega$) driving the QPCs.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on the Born and Markov approximations, and Lamb shift terms are often ignored in final simulations as they represent virtual processes.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the non-monotonic dependence of the charging rate enhancement on measurement strength parameters, and compare the performance of single, independent two-QPC, and coherent two-QPC measurement setups.
paper_structure: The paper builds from deriving the general open evolution master equation, introducing the QPC measurement Hamiltonian, analyzing the non-monotonic enhancement, and finally comparing the performance hierarchy across the three measurement configurations.
why_interesting: This work directly addresses non-equilibrium thermodynamics and measurement back-action, showing how engineered measurement coupling can optimize energy transfer processes beyond simple thermal driving.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Charger-battery setup with the charger capacitively coupled to a QPC device performing weak, continuous mea- surement.

FIG. 2. The figure on top shows the charging rate of the quantum battery as a function of the measurement strength parameters, Tmeas/Tc and µmeas/Ω. The Difference in the charging rate between the measured and unmeasured con- figurations is shown in the bottom figure. We see that the charging rate initially increases and has a maximum for cer- tain values of the measurement parameters. For higher mea- surement strengths, the charging rate is suppressed.
FIG. 3. Dependence of S(Ω) on µmeas and Tmeas. We see that the noise power of the detector is a monotonically increasing function of both measurement parameters.

FIG. 4. Independent two-QPC measurement configuration. The top panel shows the schematic of the two QPC configu- ration in which both the charger and battery qubits are in- dependently and continuously monitored. The middle panel shows the charging rate of the quantum battery as a function of Tmeas/Tc and µmeas/Ωwhile the bottom panel show the en- hancement in charging rate, ηind relative to the unmeasured baseline.

FIG. 5. Coherent two-QPC measurement configuration. The top panel shows a schematic diagram of the series-coupled QPC geometry. The middle panel shows the steady-state charging rate of the quantum battery as a function of the measurement parameters Tmeas/Tc and µmeas/Ω. The bot- tom panel shows the charging rate enhancement ηcoh = ˙E(coh) B −˙E(unmeas) B relative to the unmeasured baseline, demon- strating improvement over the single-QPC case both in terms of charging rate as well as resource requirements.
